text: We present a laparoscopic ultralow anterior resection with intersphincteric dissection and delayed coloanal anastomosis. His several advantages are displayed. This is the case of a seventy-eight years old woman who sought medical advice for fatigue and dyspnea on exertion. Patient's background shows a high risk of thrombosis and had a period-stopping cholecystectomy. Colonoscopy shows an ulcerative-putative tumor starting one centimeter from anal verge and a total length of ten centimeters. The biopsy was positive. The local staging MRI suggested a sixty-one point eight millimeters lesion with multiple adenopathies. The CT scan didn't show any other lesions. The diagnosis of a T-three M plus MO stage tumor was established.

The surgeon uses five trocars and begins the procedure from the right side of the patient. A uterine hanging technique with a straight needle suture is employed to expose the pelvis. The dissection of the mesocolon proceeds from medial to lateral, reaching and dividing the inferior mesenteric artery between clips while preserving the superior hypogastric plexus. Retroperitoneal structures are preserved using mesocolic retroperitoneal interfaces as landmarks. The dissection continues to the splenic angle, where the inferior mesenteric vein is dissected, ligated, and divided near its origin. This facilitates lateral colon mobilization, followed by the mobilization of the splenic flexure and transverse colon. Mesorectal dissection begins at the posterior wall, with care taken to preserve the inferior hypogastric plexus, and continues to the pivotal rectalis muscles. The right lateral mesorectal dissection is initiated.

The surgeon performs deeper mesorectal dissection in a circumferential manner, utilizing counter-attraction techniques to navigate the vascular planes and reach the proveractolous muscles in each quadrant.

The surgeon uses an anal retractor system to perform intersphincteric dissection starting approximately one centimeter distal in the anal mucosa, using a fine needle electrocautery in a circumferential manner. After completing the dissection at both sphincters, a purse-string suture is applied to close the distal stump. The specimen is extracted through a transanal pull-through technique under laparoscopic view, showing no tension, torsion, or inadequate perfusion from both internal and external perspectives.

The surgeon uses a hybrid energy device to dissect the specimen section line, trimming the specimen to leave approximately four centimeters of colonic tissue. Four simple stitches are placed at cardinal points to prevent retraction of the tissue. The permeability of the section is checked, and the specimen shows a total mesorectal excision with intact mesorectal fascia under microscopic examination.

As the operative period was uneventful, oral intake is restarted same evening of surgery. Five days later, second stage surgery is performed using PCEA implanted at first surgery. The stump is trimmed. Four single observable stitches are placed. Final aspect is show. Two days after second surgery, patient is discharged. Total length of stay was seven days. Final anatomopathological report is show.
